A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Baruch Spinoza

1632 – 1677

Mind and body as two attributes of one substance: parallelism, conatus, and the intellectual love of God.

A good reconstruction of Spinoza’s philosophy of mind has to begin from a striking fact: for Spinoza, the mind is neither a private inner theater nor a separable immaterial substance. But it is also not a mere by-product of matter. Mind is a real mode of the divine attribute of Thought, and every finite mind is intelligible only within the infinite order of God/Nature. His view is therefore best read as an ontologically monist, attribute-pluralist, embodied, affective, rationalist, and theological philosophy of mind. It is not Cartesian dualism, not reductive materialism, not idealism, and not ordinary epiphenomenalism. It is closer to a rigorous identity theory across attributes: one and the same individual is expressed as body under Extension and as mind or idea under Thought.

The theological dimension is not ornamental. The “mind of God,” the infinite intellect, Christ’s mind, prophetic imagination, and the question of angels all belong to the same architecture. Spinoza’s philosophical psychology is inseparable from his doctrine that the human mind is “part of the infinite intellect of God,” and his scriptural discussions of angels and prophecy are best treated as tests of his theory of imagination, representation, revelation, and finite mindedness.

I will cite the Ethics by part and proposition, for example E2p13 means Ethics, Part II, Proposition 13. I rely mainly on the mature Ethics, with the Theologico-Political Treatise and correspondence used where they bear directly on prophecy, spirits, angels, and the mind of God.

1. Terminological orientation: “soul,” “mind,” and mens

Spinoza’s main term in the Ethics is mens. It is commonly translated as “mind,” though in French and older philosophical idioms it is often rendered as âme, “soul.” A recent French discussion of De mente notes that mens covers both the cognitive and affective dimensions of mental life, so neither “mind” nor “soul” captures it perfectly if those words are understood narrowly. This is important: Spinoza’s “mind” is not merely intellect, but includes perception, imagination, desire, affect, affirmation, and self-consciousness.

Still, “soul” can mislead if it suggests a separable, immortal, Cartesian or scholastic soul-substance. Spinoza denies that the human mind is a finite substance. The human mind is a mode of Thought, specifically the idea of an actually existing body. This does not make it unreal; it makes it dependent, modal, embodied, and divine-in-context.

2. The metaphysical ground: substance, attribute, mode, God/Nature

Spinoza’s philosophy of mind cannot be extracted apart from his ontology. In Part I of the Ethics, he defines substance as what is “in itself” and conceived through itself; attribute as what the intellect perceives as constituting the essence of substance; mode as an affection or modification of substance; and God as absolutely infinite substance consisting of infinite attributes. Freedom means acting from the necessity of one’s own nature, not uncaused choice. Eternity is not long duration but existence conceived as following necessarily from the definition of an eternal thing.

From this, Spinoza argues that no substance exists or can be conceived apart from God, and that whatever exists is in God. Finite things, including human minds and bodies, are not substances but modes of God’s attributes. God acts solely by the laws of his own nature; there is no external constraint, no divine whim, and no arbitrary creation by a free will.

This already changes the philosophy of mind. A human being is not a composite of two substances, body and soul. Nor is the mind a detachable inner subject floating above nature. Human mindedness is a finite expression of divine Thought, just as the human body is a finite expression of divine Extension.

Spinoza is also careful not to anthropomorphize God. He rejects the idea that God is like a man composed of body and mind and subject to passions. Yet he does not deny divine Thought. Rather, he denies that divine Thought should be understood as a magnified human psyche. Thought is an attribute of God; intellect and will, insofar as they are determinate acts, are modes of Thought and belong to natura naturata, nature considered as what follows from God, not to natura naturans, God considered as free productive cause.

This is one of the most delicate points in Spinoza’s philosophy of mind: God is a thinking thing, but God is not a personal deliberating mind in the ordinary sense. “God’s mind” cannot mean a divine psychology with intentions, moods, choices, and passions. It means the infinite order of Thought in which the ideas of all things are contained.

3. Thought and Extension: the attribute barrier and the identity of mind and body

Spinoza’s two known attributes are Thought and Extension. Bodies are modes under Extension; ideas are modes under Thought. The key proposition is E2p7: “The order and connection of ideas is the same as the order and connection of things.” Spinoza immediately adds that whether we conceive nature under Thought, Extension, or any other attribute, we find one and the same order and one and the same causal connection.

This gives us three claims that must be held together.

First, no mode under one attribute causally acts on a mode under another attribute. Bodies do not cause ideas; ideas do not cause bodily motions. This is sometimes called the “attribute barrier.” Modern scholarship often treats this as one of Spinoza’s central commitments: the mental and physical orders are each explanatorily closed.

Second, mind and body are not two substances. They are one and the same individual expressed under two attributes. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s general article on Spinoza summarizes the point well: the human mind and human body are two expressions, under Thought and Extension, of one and the same thing, the person.

Third, because the same finite individual is expressed under different attributes, there is strict correspondence without cross-causal interaction. This is why the usual term “parallelism” is both useful and dangerous. It is useful because the order of ideas and the order of bodies correspond. It is dangerous because it can suggest two separate tracks running side by side. Spinoza’s deeper view is not two synchronized series, but one reality expressed in different attribute-registers.

Several non-English discussions are helpful here. A Spanish-language discussion in Círculo Spinoziano stresses that Spinoza’s position is not a simple theory of body-soul reflection, nor “parallelism” in the reductive sense of mental states being translations of bodily states. The real issue is the soul as idea of the body and the affect as a psycho-physical unity. A French exposition similarly emphasizes the tension between the commentator’s term “parallelism” and Spinoza’s underlying monism: there is one reality, God or Nature, structured in bodies and ideas, with no causal production of one by the other.

So, the best formula is this: the body does not cause the mind, and the mind does not cause the body; rather, mind and body are the same finite individual expressed under different attributes.

4. The mind as the idea of the body

Spinoza’s central doctrine of the human mind is E2p13: “The object of the idea constituting the human mind is the body, or a certain mode of Extension which actually exists, and nothing else.” Before that, E2p11 says the first element constituting the actual being of the human mind is the idea of a particular actually existing thing, and its corollary says that the human mind is part of the infinite intellect of God.

The human mind is therefore not a substance, not an immaterial organ, and not a faculty behind ideas. It is an idea: more precisely, the idea of the human body. Since the human body is complex, the human mind is also complex. E2p15 says the mind is composed of many ideas, corresponding to the many things that compose and affect the body. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that for Spinoza every finite mode of Extension has a corresponding mode of Thought, and composite bodies have composite ideas; the human mind is simply the idea corresponding to the human body, though the human body’s complexity gives it special perceptual capacities.

This doctrine is the root of Spinoza’s embodied philosophy of mind. To know what a mind is, we must know what kind of body it is the idea of. A richer, more complex, more integrated, more capable body has a richer, more capable mind. Spinoza explicitly says that as a body is more capable of acting and being acted on in many ways at once, its mind is more capable of perceiving many things at once. The human mind’s excellence depends on the human body’s structure and capacities.

This does not mean that the body explains the mind by cross-attribute causation. Rather, the body individuates the mind’s object. The mind is not caused by its body; it is the idea of its body.

5. Universal mindedness: are all things “animated”?

Spinoza’s famous scholium to E2p13 says that all individuals are “animated,” though in different degrees. This is often treated as Spinoza’s panpsychism or universal mindedness. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that, for Spinoza, mindedness extends at least to all “individuals” or composite entities, because a finite mind is a part or component idea of the divine infinite intellect representing a discernible bit of being.

But this must be handled carefully. Spinoza is not saying that stones think like people, that planets deliberate, or that every thing has a little human soul inside it. He is saying that every finite individual, insofar as it is a determinate mode under Extension, has an idea under Thought. The degree and type of mindedness depends on the structure and power of the corresponding body.

A valuable Spanish-language interpretation by Vidal Peña warns against reading Spinoza’s “all things are animated” as a claim that things are animated by “spirits.” Peña reads the doctrine as a thesis about the objective intelligibility of reality: there are ideas of all things in God; these ideas are not subjective products of a personal mind but ideal structures corresponding to the essences of things. On this reading, Spinoza’s so-called pananimation means that individualized things have intelligible essences and places in the infinite order of Thought, not that every object has a human-like inner life.

This interpretation is especially useful for a comprehensive philosophy of mind. It lets us distinguish three levels:

There is universal ideationality: every thing has an idea in God.

There is finite mindedness: every individual has a corresponding mind in proportion to its bodily organization.

There is human reflective consciousness: a high-degree, complex form of mindedness tied to the powers of the human body, language, memory, imagination, and reason.

Spinoza’s theory is thus panpsychist only in a qualified sense. It is better called graded universal mindedness or universal ideational monism.

6. Perception, self-knowledge, and the opacity of the body

Because the mind is the idea of the body, the mind perceives what happens in the body. E2p12 says that whatever takes place in the object of the idea constituting the human mind must be perceived by the human mind. But this does not imply conscious omniscience about every bodily event. Much of what the mind “perceives” is confused, partial, and non-reflective. Spinoza later says the mind knows the body only through ideas of the body’s modifications.

Modern interpreters often call this the “pancreas problem”: if the mind perceives whatever happens in its body, why are we not aware of all organ-level events? The answer is that perception is not the same as clear, conscious, adequate awareness. The mind has ideas corresponding to bodily states, but many are confused, indistinct, or functionally integrated into the whole body’s activity without becoming explicit awareness.

This gives Spinoza an early and powerful account of bodily opacity. We are embodied beings, but not transparent to ourselves. We know our body primarily through affections: through how our body is modified in encounters with other bodies. Our knowledge of ourselves and external things begins from bodily-affective mediation, not from a pure inner access.

The same applies to self-consciousness. E2p20–21 introduces the “idea of the mind,” or idea of an idea. If a person knows something, Spinoza says, he knows that he knows, and knows that he knows that he knows, and so on. But this reflexivity is not a Cartesian ego standing outside the chain of nature. It is another mode of Thought: the idea of an idea.

Consciousness in Spinoza is best understood as scalar and achievement-like. It increases with bodily and mental power. E5p39s says that a child or infant, whose body is capable of few activities and heavily dependent on external causes, has a mind scarcely conscious of itself, God, or things; a body capable of many activities corresponds to a mind highly conscious of itself, God, and things.

7. Ideas are not images, words, or mental pictures

Spinoza’s philosophy of mind requires a sharp distinction between ideas, images, and words. In E2p49s he warns that people confuse ideas with the images of things formed by bodily contact, or with words used to signify things. An idea is not an “inanimate picture on a panel.” It is a mode of Thought that involves affirmation or negation.

This is crucial. Spinoza is not offering a picture-theory of mind. Ideas are not internal visual replicas. Images are bodily traces or modifications; imagination is the mental awareness of those bodily images; words are bodily-linguistic signs; ideas are acts of thinking with affirmative structure.

This lets Spinoza explain why a false idea is not a positively false object in the mind. Falsity is privation: the lack of adequate knowledge involved in partial and confused ideas. All ideas, insofar as they are referred to God, are true; they become inadequate only relative to a finite mind that possesses them partially.

A German article by Johannes Haag, Spinoza über die Intentionalität geistiger Zustände, is useful here because it frames Spinoza’s theory of ideas as relevant to a naturalized account of intentionality. Haag argues that Spinoza’s handling of intentionality can distinguish intentional from non-intentional representation and that this helps avoid treating Spinoza’s concept of idea as simply ambiguous.

From a contemporary point of view, Spinoza’s theory of intentionality can be reconstructed like this: an idea represents its object not because it resembles it as a picture, nor because the object causes it across attributes, but because it occupies a determinate place in the order of Thought corresponding to the object’s place in the order of things. Human perception of external bodies is indirect: ideas of bodily affections involve both the nature of the human body and the nature of the external body, but they indicate our body’s state more than the external thing’s intrinsic nature.

8. Imagination, memory, and error

Spinoza’s imagination is broader than what we usually call imagination. It includes sense experience, image-based cognition, memory, association, testimony, and sign-based thought. Imagination arises from the body’s affections and their traces.

Memory is an association of ideas corresponding to the association of bodily modifications. Spinoza’s example is linguistic: a Roman hearing pomum moves from the sound to the fruit because the body has often been affected by the word and the fruit together. A soldier seeing horse-tracks may think of cavalry and war, while a farmer may think of ploughs and fields. Memory is therefore embodied association, not a purely intellectual storehouse.

Imagination is not simply bad. It is indispensable for finite life. But it is the main source of error when taken as adequate knowledge. Because the human body is limited, it can form only a limited number of distinct images at once; when images exceed that capacity, they become confused. General terms such as “man,” “horse,” or “dog” often arise from such confused bodily-imaginative grouping, varying from person to person according to habitual bodily experience.

This has major consequences for philosophy of mind. Spinoza explains cognitive bias, abstraction, linguistic confusion, perceptual error, and social disagreement through the embodied limits of imaginative cognition. He also explains why truth does not automatically erase imagination. Knowing the sun’s true distance does not stop it from appearing near. A true idea does not annihilate the bodily image; it changes our relation to it.

9. The three kinds of knowledge

Spinoza distinguishes three kinds of cognition.

The first kind is imagination or opinion: knowledge from random experience, sensory images, signs, words, and testimony. It is fragmentary and often confused.

The second kind is reason: knowledge through common notions and adequate ideas of what is common to things. Common notions are not arbitrary abstractions; they are grounded in real common features of bodies and allow adequate cognition.

The third kind is intuitive knowledge, scientia intuitiva: knowledge proceeding from an adequate idea of God’s attributes to adequate knowledge of the essence of particular things. It is not mystical irrationalism. It is the highest intellectual grasp of things as following from God/Nature.

Spinoza lays out the first, second, and third kinds of knowledge in E2p40s2, and identifies reason and intuition as necessarily true. Modern scholarship similarly emphasizes that Spinoza’s epistemology is governed by universal intelligibility, the explanatory closure of mental and physical orders, and the priority of an infinite thinker over finite minds.

The philosophy-of-mind consequence is that mental life is not exhausted by consciousness or representation. It is also structured by degrees of adequacy. A mind becomes more active, more powerful, and freer as its ideas become more adequate.

10. Affects: mind and body as one affective individual

Part III of the Ethics is a naturalistic theory of affect. Spinoza rejects the idea that human beings are a “kingdom within a kingdom.” The emotions follow the same laws of nature as everything else. He says he will treat human actions and desires as if dealing with lines, planes, and solids.

An affect is both a bodily modification and the idea of that modification. More precisely, emotions are modifications of the body by which its power of acting is increased or diminished, together with the ideas of those modifications. If we are the adequate cause of such a modification, the affect is an action; otherwise it is a passion.

Here Spinoza’s mind-body theory becomes an affect theory. Affects are not merely subjective feelings added onto bodily processes. Nor are they merely physiological events. They are psycho-physical modes of the one individual: bodily changes in power, expressed mentally as ideas of those changes.

Spinoza’s basic affective triad is desire, joy, and sadness. Desire is appetite together with consciousness of it; joy is transition to greater power; sadness is transition to lesser power. Love is joy accompanied by the idea of an external cause; hate is sadness accompanied by the idea of an external cause. The general definition of an emotion as a passion is a confused idea by which the mind affirms a greater or lesser force of existence of its body or a part of it.

This is one of Spinoza’s deepest contributions to philosophy of mind: cognition and affect are not separate faculties. Affects are ideas, but ideas that express changes in bodily power. Mental life is evaluative from within because the mind is the idea of a body striving to persevere.

11. Conatus, desire, and the actual essence of the mind

Spinoza’s doctrine of conatus is the core of his psychology. Each thing, insofar as it is in itself, strives to persevere in its being; this striving is nothing other than the thing’s actual essence. The mind, whether it has clear or confused ideas, strives to persist and is conscious of this striving.

For human beings, this striving is experienced as appetite and desire. Desire is not a secondary preference added to cognition; it is the conscious expression of our essence as striving. This means Spinoza’s philosophy of mind is motivational at its foundation. Minds are not passive containers of representations. They are modes of striving, structured by ideas and affects.

This also explains Spinoza’s rejection of final causes. What people call an end or final cause is really desire considered as the cause of action. Humans are conscious of their appetites but ignorant of the causes that determine those appetites.

The result is a non-teleological but deeply purposive psychology. Nature has no external purposes, but finite individuals strive. Mental life is the ideational side of that striving.

12. Will, affirmation, and the denial of free will

Spinoza denies free will in the libertarian sense. The mind has no absolute faculty of willing. There are only particular volitions, and these are identical with particular ideas. E2p49 says that there is no volition or affirmation and negation in the mind except that which an idea involves; its corollary says that will and understanding are one and the same.

This is not a denial of agency. It is a denial of uncaused choice and faculty psychology. A mental decision is not an intervention by a free will into a causal chain. It is an idea-affect within the order of Thought, corresponding to a bodily state under Extension.

Spinoza’s famous critique in E3p2 says that body cannot determine mind to think, nor can mind determine body to motion or rest. People believe they freely decide because they are conscious of their actions but ignorant of the causes determining them. He also remarks that no one has yet determined what the body can do, a phrase often taken as one of his most powerful anti-Cartesian insights.

Freedom, for Spinoza, is not indeterminacy. It is activity through adequate ideas. A mind is active insofar as it has adequate ideas and passive insofar as it has inadequate ideas. This transforms the philosophy of agency: freedom is understanding necessity and acting from the necessity of one’s own nature, rather than being driven by external causes through confused ideas.

13. Bondage, therapy, and the power of understanding

Part IV defines human bondage as our inability to moderate and check the emotions. When we are dominated by passions, we are not our own masters; we may see the better and follow the worse.

Good and evil are not intrinsic metaphysical properties. Good is what we certainly know to be useful to us; evil is what hinders us from attaining a good. Virtue and power are the same: virtue is our essence insofar as it has the power to act according to its own nature.

Spinoza is psychologically realistic. An emotion cannot be overcome merely by a true proposition considered abstractly. It can only be controlled or destroyed by a contrary and stronger emotion. This is why Spinoza’s ethics is also a practical psychology: reason must become affectively powerful. Adequate knowledge produces active affects; it does not merely issue commands.

Part V gives the “remedies” for the passions. Since the power of the mind is defined by understanding, the remedies involve forming clear and distinct ideas of affects, separating affects from confused ideas of external causes, associating images according to the intellectual order, understanding necessity, and referring bodily modifications to the idea of God.

This is not mind-over-body voluntarism. Spinoza explicitly rejects Descartes’s pineal-gland model and the fantasy of absolute mental dominion over bodily passions. But because the order of ideas and the order of bodily modifications correspond, the intellectual reordering of ideas corresponds to a bodily reordering of images and affective dispositions.

In modern terms, Spinoza’s therapy is cognitive-affective reorganization under a non-dualist metaphysics.

14. The mind of God and the infinite intellect

The “mind of God” must be treated with great care. Spinoza does say that God is a thinking thing and that the idea of God, from which infinitely many things follow, exists in God. But he also argues that intellect, whether finite or infinite, is a mode of Thought and belongs to natura naturata, not to God’s essence considered as natura naturans.

So there are two levels.

At the level of attribute, God is Thought itself, infinite power of thinking.

At the level of mode, the infinite intellect is the infinite ordered system of ideas: the idea of God and the ideas of all things following from divine nature.

Finite minds are parts of this infinite intellect. When the human mind perceives something, Spinoza says this means that God has the idea, not insofar as God is infinite absolutely, but insofar as God constitutes the essence of that human mind. When the idea is inadequate, God has more of the relevant causal-intellectual content than the finite mind does.

This is the deepest sense in which Spinoza’s philosophy of mind is theological. A finite mind is not self-standing. It is a partial expression of divine Thought. Adequate knowledge is not private possession but participation in the infinite intellect’s adequate order.

The Theologico-Political Treatise uses language of the “Spirit of God” and explores the Hebrew ruagh, which can mean wind, breath, life, disposition, passion, mind, or spirit. Spinoza says scriptural phrases such as the Spirit of God must be interpreted carefully according to context. He also says that the “mind of God” and God’s eternal thoughts are impressed on our minds, which fits the Ethics doctrine that finite minds are modes within the divine order of Thought.

But this must not be psychologized. Spinoza explicitly says we can understand God but cannot imagine God. In his letter to Boxel, he says he has as clear an idea of God as of a triangle in the intellectual sense, but not as clear a mental image, because God cannot be imagined.

Thus, “God’s mind” is not imaginable as a superhuman consciousness. It is intelligible as infinite Thought.

15. God’s love, human blessedness, and the intellectual love of God

Spinoza denies that God has passions. God is not affected by joy or sadness, and strictly speaking God does not love or hate anyone in the ordinary affective sense. Yet Part V also says that God loves himself with an infinite intellectual love, and that the human mind’s intellectual love of God is part of the infinite love by which God loves himself, insofar as God is explained through the essence of the human mind under the form of eternity.

This is not a contradiction if we distinguish passive affect from intellectual activity. God has no passions because God cannot pass to greater or lesser perfection. But intellectual love is not a passive emotional fluctuation. It is the active joy of adequate understanding.

For the human mind, the intellectual love of God arises from the third kind of knowledge. It is the mind’s highest blessedness: understanding itself, its body, and things through God/Nature, and thereby participating consciously in divine self-understanding.

This gives Spinoza a theological philosophy of mind in a strong sense: the highest mental state is not merely accurate representation, but active participation in the infinite intellect’s knowledge of itself.

16. Death, eternity, and the non-personal survival of mind

Spinoza’s doctrine of the eternity of the mind is difficult but essential. He denies ordinary personal immortality based on memory, imagination, or the survival of an autobiographical soul. E5p21 says the mind can imagine or remember what is past only while the body endures.

Yet E5p22–23 says that in God there is necessarily an idea expressing the essence of the human body under the form of eternity, and that the human mind cannot be absolutely destroyed with the body; something of it remains that is eternal. This eternity is not duration. It cannot be defined by time and has no relation to memory of pre-existence or postmortem biography.

This doctrine is often misunderstood as a reintroduction of a disembodied soul. But Spinoza’s own terms prevent that. The eternal part of the mind is still tied to the essence of the body, conceived under eternity. The mind’s eternity is not the mind floating free of embodiment; it is the eternal idea of the body’s essence in God.

Modern scholarship has emphasized this point: Spinoza’s eternity doctrine is not traditional personal immortality; memories and sense-dependent features do not persist beyond death.

The practical consequence is that the more the mind understands by reason and intuition, the more of it is eternal, in the relevant sense. E5p39 says that one whose body is capable of many activities has a mind highly conscious of itself, God, and things; the eternal part of the mind is understanding, while the perishing part is imagination.

So Spinoza’s “immortality” is not survival of the empirical self. It is the mind’s participation in eternal understanding.

17. Prophecy and religious imagination

The Theologico-Political Treatise is philosophically important because it applies Spinoza’s theory of mind to Scripture, prophecy, imagination, and revelation.

Spinoza argues that prophecy is not primarily a function of superior philosophical intellect. Prophets have vivid imagination. Revelation generally occurs through words, images, figures, dreams, visions, and signs. No one except Christ receives revelation without the aid of imagination.

This is not a contemptuous dismissal of prophecy. It is a classification of prophetic cognition. Prophecy belongs to imagination, not to adequate philosophical demonstration. But imagination can still have enormous ethical and political power. It can move people, shape obedience, communicate moral truths, and organize communal life.

A Spanish article on the Theologico-Political Treatise and its Judeo-Arabic background highlights this point: for Spinoza, revelation does not convey speculative philosophical truths through reason; prophets perceive revelations through imagination, by words or images, and Scripture is primarily practical rather than speculative.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Spinoza does not reduce religion to error. He treats scriptural imagination as a real and powerful mental mode. It is inadequate as metaphysical science, but not worthless as ethical communication.

18. Christ’s mind

Christ occupies a special place. Spinoza says in the TTP that God communicated to Christ immediately, “mind to mind,” not through the ordinary prophetic mediation of imagination. Christ alone, he says, seems to have received revelation without words or visions.

Later, Spinoza says Christ must be taken to have had a clear and adequate perception; he was not so much a prophet as the “mouthpiece of God.” Christ perceived what was revealed truly and adequately, and those who have the “mind of Christ” perceive the laws of God as eternal truths.

This is a crucial passage for a comprehensive Spinozist philosophy of mind. Christ’s mind represents, within Spinoza’s scriptural hermeneutics, the highest finite religious cognition: not imaginative prophecy merely, but adequate intellectual perception of divine law as eternal truth. Whether one reads this theologically, philosophically, or both, it shows that Spinoza’s hierarchy of minds includes a special case of extraordinary adequate cognition.

19. Angels and angelic minds

Spinoza does not offer a full positive angelology in the Ethics. His ontology leaves no room for finite immaterial substances existing alongside God. Since no substance exists outside God, and finite things are modes, an angel cannot be a separate spiritual substance in the traditional Cartesian-scholastic sense.

But this does not mean that angelic language is unimportant. In the TTP, Spinoza repeatedly treats angels as part of the scriptural economy of revelation. Sometimes “angel” names a figure or vision through which God’s message is imaginatively represented. He discusses scriptural angelic appearances such as an angel bearing a sword, and he rejects forced interpretations that make all such appearances dreams simply to fit an external philosophical system.

At other times, scriptural “angels” are natural powers described in religious language. Spinoza notes that in Psalm 104, wind and fire are called angels and ministers of God, and he generalizes that divine decree, commandment, fiat, and word often express the action and order of nature.

There are therefore at least four Spinozist readings of angels.

First, angels may be imaginative-revelatory figures: appearances, voices, or visions by which a prophet receives or represents divine communication.

Second, angels may be natural powers or events described through the devotional language of Scripture.

Third, angels may be “created voices” or mediating signs, not independent metaphysical persons. Spinoza says God revealed through Christ as he had before done through angels, glossing angels there as a created voice, visions, and similar media.

Fourth, if one asks whether an angel could be a real finite mind, Spinoza’s Ethics gives a conditional answer: any finite individual, if it exists as a determinate mode, has a corresponding idea under Thought; its mind would be the idea of its body or extended individual structure. But a purely disembodied finite angelic mind would not fit Spinoza’s mature doctrine that a mind is the idea of a body.

The Boxel correspondence on ghosts and spirits is relevant here, though it is not directly about angels. Boxel asks about apparitions and spirits; Spinoza responds politely but insists that before affirming such beings, one must say what intelligible properties they have. If “ghost” merely names things we do not understand, Spinoza will not deny that unknown things exist, but he will not accept unintelligible spirit-talk as knowledge. In a later reply he says ghosts have no intelligible property attributed to them and seem like phantoms no one can understand.

Applied to angels, this gives a respectful but disciplined conclusion. Spinoza need not dismiss angels as “theological distractions.” Rather, he asks: under what attribute, in what order of causation, with what intelligible structure, and through what mode of cognition are angels being discussed? In Scripture, angels often belong to the imagination and to the symbolic-revelatory mediation of divine order. In strict metaphysics, any real angelic mind would have to be a finite mode of Thought corresponding to some finite individual under Extension. The traditional idea of a wholly disembodied finite intellect is therefore difficult to accommodate, but angelic mediation remains philosophically important as a case study in imagination, revelation, and the limits of finite cognition.

20. What kind of philosophy of mind emerges?

Here is the systematic extraction.

Spinoza’s philosophy of mind begins with divine immanence. Every mind is in God because every idea is a mode of divine Thought. The human mind is not an isolated subject but a partial expression of the infinite intellect.

It is a mind-body identity theory, but not a reductive physicalism. Mind and body are one and the same individual expressed under Thought and Extension. Mental causation is closed within Thought; bodily causation is closed within Extension. The body does not produce the mind, and the mind does not push the body.

It is an embodied theory of mind. The human mind is the idea of the human body. The mind’s powers depend on the body’s powers: complexity, integration, capacity to act, capacity to be affected, and capacity to preserve organized activity.

It is a graded theory of mindedness. All individuals are animated in different degrees because every individual has an idea in God. But only some minds have complex consciousness, memory, language, reason, and intuitive knowledge.

It is a representational theory without pictorialism. Ideas are not images. Images are bodily traces; words are signs; ideas are affirmative modes of Thought. Representation arises through the order of ideas, not through resemblance or cross-attribute causation.

It is a theory of consciousness as reflexive and scalar. Consciousness involves ideas of ideas and increases with the mind’s power. It is not an all-or-nothing property present equally in all beings.

It is a theory of error as privation. Falsehood is not a positive mental object but the incompleteness of partial, confused ideas in finite minds.

It is an affective theory of mind. Affects are ideas of bodily power-changes. Desire, joy, and sadness are fundamental to mental life. Cognition and affect are not separate compartments.

It is a conative theory. Each mind strives to persevere in being. Desire is conscious striving. Mental life is fundamentally active or passive depending on whether it is determined by adequate or inadequate ideas.

It is anti-libertarian but not anti-agency. Free will as uncaused choice is an illusion. True freedom is activity from adequate understanding.

It is therapeutic. The mind becomes freer by forming adequate ideas of affects, understanding necessity, weakening confused attachments, and referring things to God/Nature.

It is theological. The infinite intellect is not an optional doctrine but the ground of finite minds. The mind’s highest state is intellectual love of God, in which finite understanding participates in divine self-understanding.

It is non-standard about immortality. The autobiographical self does not survive as memory or imagination; nevertheless, the mind has an eternal aspect insofar as it understands the body’s essence and things under the form of eternity.

It is hermeneutically sensitive to religious cognition. Prophecy, angels, and the mind of Christ are not distractions but important cases in Spinoza’s classification of imagination, adequate understanding, and divine communication.

21. Main tensions and interpretive problems

Several tensions remain and should be preserved rather than flattened.

The first is the relation between “mind as idea of body” and “mind-body identity.” If the mind is identical with the body under another attribute, how can it also represent the body as its object? Contemporary scholarship recognizes this as one of the central puzzles of Spinoza’s theory of mind.

The second is panpsychism. If every individual has a mind, does the concept of mind become too broad? The strongest answer is the graded one: all individuals have ideational correlates, but not all have human-like consciousness.

The third is God’s intellect. Spinoza says God is a thinking thing, yet he also says intellect, even infinite intellect, belongs to nature considered as produced, not to God’s essence as productive nature. This requires distinguishing God’s attribute Thought from the infinite intellect as a mode of Thought.

The fourth is eternity of the mind. Spinoza denies personal memory-survival but affirms something eternal of the mind. The best reconstruction is that the eternal part is the mind’s adequate understanding, grounded in the idea of the body’s essence under eternity.

The fifth is angels. Traditional disembodied angelic intellects do not fit easily into Spinoza’s system, but scriptural angels remain philosophically significant as imaginative mediators, natural powers in religious language, or possible finite individuals only if understood as modes with corresponding bodily/extended structure.

22. Final formulation

Spinoza’s philosophy of mind can be stated in one dense formula:

A mind is a finite mode of divine Thought, constituted as the idea of a finite body, internally ordered by the same causal structure as that body but explained only through Thought; its passive life consists in inadequate ideas of bodily affections, its active life in adequate ideas, its striving in desire, its freedom in understanding necessity, its blessedness in intellectual love of God, and its eternity in participation in the infinite intellect through knowledge of things under the form of eternity.

That formula includes the human soul, animal and non-human mindedness, consciousness, affect, cognition, agency, religious imagination, divine mind, Christ’s mind, and the constrained but meaningful place of angels. It is a philosophy of mind in which psychology, metaphysics, ethics, and theology are not separable disciplines but different perspectives on one order of Nature.

PreviousRené Descartes NextJohn Locke

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
